title: -0.0041 BPB by Reordering Training Data (Curriculum Learning)
author: abaybektursun
body: ## Non-record: Shard Reordering by Model Perplexity (3-seed mean val_bpb=1.1187) **val_bpb = 1.1187** (3-seed mean) | ~15.9 MB | 8xH100 SXM, 600s + Legal TTT Built on [PR #549](https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/549) (merged #1, val_bpb=1.1220). Single change: reorder training shards so the model sees the hardest ones first. ### Results | Seed | PR #549 (sequential) | This (hardest-first) | Delta | |------|---------------------|---------------------|-------| | 1337 | 1.1217 | **1.1183** | **-0.0034** | | 42 | 1.1222 | **1.1181** | **-0.0041** | | 2025 | 1.1221 | **1.1198** | **-0.0023** | | **Mean** | **1.1220** | **1.1187** | **-0.0033** | ### Run Command ```bash SHARD_ORDER=44,63,65,42,18,67,30,69,61,3,13,19,50,49,56,45,73,79,57,32,\ 28,68,66,34,46,38,17,77,0,14,26,74,59,62,41,9,58,22,78,4,48,8,12,27,75,\ 36,16,43,52,15,33,47,25,55,54,23,37,51,31,21,60,1,20,72,24,53,39,35,71,\ 76,40,5,10,2,7,6,70,11,64,29 \ torchrun --standalone --nproc_per_node=8 train_gpt.py ``` --- # The Full Story I beat my own #1 record without changing the model — just the order I read the data. --- ## The question At 83ms/step I get ~7,200 steps in 600s. That's 5.66B tokens out of 10B — **57% of the dataset**. Shards 0 through ~45 get seen. Shards 46-79 never do. Everyone trains sequentially. I wanted to know if the order matters. --- ## Ranking shards by model difficulty I trained a small model (6L, 512d) for 500 steps on shard 0, then scored all 80 shards by cross-entropy loss. High loss = unfamiliar patterns = most to teach. Range across shards: **0.0475**. Shard 44 (loss 4.98) contains patterns genuinely harder for the model than shard 29 (loss 4.93). Not noise — signal. --- ## Where the hard shards hide Hard and easy shards are scattered randomly across the dataset. No positional pattern. Shard 44 (hardest, #1) sits next to shard 43 (#48). Sequential ordering isn't optimized for anything except being the default. --- ## Every seed improves For context: my last three PRs each took days of architecture and quantization work to gain 0.001-0.003 BPB. --- ## Plot twist: easiest-first works too I ran the reverse ordering (easiest shards first) expecting it to be worse. It isn't. | Order | Post-TTT BPB (seed 1337) | |-------|-------------------------| | Sequential (default) | 1.1217 | | **Hardest first** | **1.1183** | | **Easiest first** | **1.1186** | | Random shuffle | *(running)* | Both informed orderings crush sequential. The gap between hardest-first and easiest-first is 0.0003 — noise. The gap between either and sequential is 0.003+ — signal. This changes the interpretation entirely. The improvement isn't from curriculum learning. It's from **breaking whatever accidental structure is baked into sequential shard numbering.** The default order isn't random — it reflects whatever process created the shards. Reordering by any principled criterion destroys that structure, and apparently that structure was hurting. The random shuffle experiment will tell us if even an unprincipled reordering helps. If it does, the conclusion is stark: the sequential default is actively harmful. --- ## The method **Step 1**: Train a small model (6L, 512d) for 500 steps on shard 0. ~40 seconds, single GPU. **Step 2**: Score all 80 shards by cross-entropy loss. **Step 3**: Sort by loss descending (hardest first). Pass via env var. The ranking script (`score_shards.py`) is included. The entire diff from PR #549 is 6 lines in `TokenStream.__init__`. --- ## How far could this go? This used the crudest possible ranking — a tiny model trained for 500 steps on a single shard, scoring at whole-shard granularity (125M tokens per unit). The signal is blunt. A properly trained embedding model with a smarter objective would find sharper signal. Ranking individual sequences instead of whole shards (125M → 2K granularity) would be far more precise. Iterative re-ranking — scoring shards every 1,000 steps as the model's weaknesses shift — would adapt the curriculum to the model in real time. No validation data is used anywhere in this pipeline. The ranking model trains on training data, scores training data, and the ordering is fixed before the real training begins. Every token the model is evaluated on is scored by a model that has never seen it. No rules broken, no eval data touched. I thought it was interesting enough to share. --- ### Credits - Original inspiration: Lucas Fievet - Base model: [PR #549](https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/549) by @abaybektursun
submission_readme:
# You're Training on 57% of Your Data. Does It Matter Which 57%?

**Non-record submission: a case study on training data ordering.**

Everyone in this competition trains sequentially through the shards. Nobody questions the order. We did — and found a free **-0.0033 BPB** improvement by reordering shards based on model perplexity. Zero architecture changes, zero hyperparameter tuning, ten lines of code.

## The Setup

At 83ms/step we get ~7,200 steps in 600s. That's 5.66B tokens out of 10B — **57% of the dataset**. Shards 0 through ~45 get seen. Shards 46-79 never do.

## Token Statistics Say It Doesn't Matter

We computed KL(val || shard) for all 80 training shards. Every shard has essentially the same token distribution. Range: 0.0009. Translates to ~0.00005 BPB. Dead end.

![Token frequencies vs model perplexity](figures/fig1_kl_vs_perplexity.png)

Left panel. All shards look identical by token frequency.

## The Model Disagrees

Trained a model 500 steps on one shard, then scored all 80 shards by cross-entropy loss. Right panel above. Range: **0.0475** — 100× larger than the KL signal. The shards are NOT all the same. Token statistics just can't see the difference.

As expected — KL counts tokens, the model scores sequences. But the magnitude of the gap is what matters: the model finds **100× more variation** than token statistics do.

![KL rank vs perplexity rank](figures/fig4_the_insight.png)

**r = -0.056.** The two metrics are uncorrelated. The shard most similar to val by token frequency is middling by model difficulty.

## Where the Hard Shards Are

The difficulty is about content, not position. Hard and easy shards are scattered randomly across the dataset.

![Shard difficulty heatmap](figures/fig2_shard_heatmap.png)

Shard 44 (hardest, rank #1) sits next to shard 43 (rank #48). Sequential ordering — the default in every training framework — isn't optimized for anything except simplicity.

![Sequential vs optimal ordering](figures/fig3_sequential_vs_optimal.png)

## Results: 3-Seed Validated

Reran our merged #1 submission (PR #549) with shards reordered hardest-first. Same code, same hyperparameters, same compute budget.

| Seed | Sequential (PR #549) | Hardest-first | Delta |
|------|---------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1337 | 1.1217 | **1.1183** | **-0.0034** |
| 42 | 1.1222 | **1.1181** | **-0.0041** |
| 2025 | 1.1221 | **1.1198** | **-0.0023** |
| **Mean** | **1.1220** | **1.1187** | **-0.0033** |

Every seed improves. Mean improvement: **-0.0033 BPB.**

![Three-seed comparison](figures/fig5_three_seed_comparison.png)

![Improvement consistency and cost](figures/fig6_delta_consistency.png)

For context: our last three PRs each took days of architecture and quantization work to gain 0.001-0.003 BPB. This took ten lines of code.

## The Change

```python
class TokenStream:
    def __init__(self, pattern: str):
        self.files = [Path(p) for p in sorted(glob.glob(pattern))]
        # NEW: reorder shards by model difficulty (hardest first)
        shard_order = os.environ.get("SHARD_ORDER", "")
        if shard_order:
            order = [int(x) for x in shard_order.split(",")]
            reordered = [self.files[i] for i in order if i < len(self.files)]
            remaining = [f for i, f in enumerate(self.files) if i not in set(order)]
            self.files = reordered + remaining
```

```bash
SHARD_ORDER=44,63,65,42,18,67,30,69,61,3,13,19,50,49,56,45,73,79,57,32,\
28,68,66,34,46,38,17,77,0,14,26,74,59,62,41,9,58,22,78,4,48,8,12,27,75,\
36,16,43,52,15,33,47,25,55,54,23,37,51,31,21,60,1,20,72,24,53,39,35,71,\
76,40,5,10,2,7,6,70,11,64,29
```

## Method: How We Ranked Shards

1. Train a 6-layer, 512d model for 500 steps on shard 0 (single GPU, ~40 seconds)
2. Score all 80 shards by cross-entropy loss with this partially-trained model
3. Sort shards by loss descending (hardest first)
4. Pass the ordering via `SHARD_ORDER` environment variable

The ranking model is deliberately small and undertrained — it captures which shards have patterns the model hasn't learned yet. A fully-trained model would rank differently (everything is "easy" by then).

## Open Questions

- **Adaptive curriculum**: Re-rank shards every 1,000 steps as the model learns. The optimal ordering probably changes during training.
- **Anti-curriculum**: We haven't tested easiest-first. It might help build foundations before tackling hard patterns. *(Experiment running.)*
- **Transfer across architectures**: Our ranking was done with a 6-layer model. Does it transfer to 11-layer? To different hyperparameters?
- **Interaction with SWA/EMA**: Does the ordering effect survive weight averaging?

## Credits

- Original idea (train on similar data): Lucas Fievet
- Base model: [PR #549](https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/549) by @abaybektursun (merged #1)
- Analysis and implementation: @abaybektursun
